cards.front: How long is an IPv6 address, and how is it written?
cards.back: 128 bits, written as 8 groups of 4 hex digits separated by colons. Example: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:ff00:0042:8329. Compression rules let you shrink it to 2001:db8::ff00:42:8329.

(1) Drop leading zeros in each hextet (0042 → 42, 0000 → 0). (2) Replace ONE contiguous run of all-zero hextets with `::` (used at most once per address). Example: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 → 2001:db8::1.

Primary: IPv4 address exhaustion (4.3 billion addresses, mostly already allocated). Secondary: simplified header (fixed 40 bytes), built-in security (IPsec native), no fragmentation by routers, no broadcasts, multicast/anycast first-class, SLAAC for stateless addressing.

cards.front: What's IPv6's equivalent of ARP, and what protocol family does it use?
cards.back: Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP), part of ICMPv6. Uses NS (Neighbor Solicitation) and NA (Neighbor Advertisement) messages — sent to multicast addresses, NOT broadcast (IPv6 has no broadcast). Also handles Router Solicitation/Advertisement (RS/RA), redirect, and DAD.

Duplicate Address Detection. Before using a new IPv6 address, the host sends an NS for that address. If anyone responds with an NA, the address is in use → don't claim it. Default behavior, runs automatically on IPv6 stack startup.

cards.front: How do you enable IPv6 routing globally on a Cisco router?
cards.back: `ipv6 unicast-routing`. Without this command, IPv6 packets are dropped (not forwarded) even if interfaces have IPv6 addresses.

cards.front: Name the 5 Regional Internet Registries (RIRs) and which region each covers.
cards.back: AFRINIC: Africa. APNIC: Asia-Pacific. ARIN: USA, Canada, parts of Caribbean and North Atlantic. LACNIC: Latin America and Caribbean. RIPE NCC: Europe, Middle East, parts of Central Asia. They allocate IP blocks (IPv4 + IPv6) to ISPs and large orgs, working under IANA.

cards.front: Standard IPv6 prefix sizes: enterprise allocation, single subnet?
cards.back: An enterprise typically receives a /48 from its ISP/RIR (allows 65,536 /64 subnets). A single IPv6 subnet uses /64 by default — required for SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration), which generates a 64-bit interface ID. Larger blocks: ISPs receive /32; very large orgs may get /29 or larger.

cards.front: How do you assign an IPv6 address to a Cisco router interface?
cards.back: Per interface: `ipv6 address 2001:db8:1::1/64` (specify full address + prefix length). For EUI-64 auto-derived host portion: `ipv6 address 2001:db8:1::/64 eui-64`. To use only a link-local: `ipv6 enable`. Globally first: `ipv6 unicast-routing` (otherwise IPv6 packets won't be forwarded).
